sentences.text: About the concentration camps in general?
Well, we knew there were concentration camps.
We knew there was a large ghetto in-- in Warsaw, of course, which was just a concentration camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Otherwise I do not really remember.
I knew that the concentration camp in Plaszow was being built.
Can I talk about this now?
The--
Yeah.
Well, the Jewish Council had a very difficult role.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They formed them, as I have in one of my papers that I have, the gestapo, or the Jewish-- the German authorities formed them to protect the interests of the Jewish population-- I'll put this in quotation marks-- and also to make sure that the Jewish population followed the-- [INAUDIBLE] orders of the Germans.
The Jewish Judenrat, as it called, was-- I think consisted of 25 people in Krakow.
Most of them were prominent-- prominent business people or-- or education-- education.
I remember, the one-- the first president that the Judenrat had was a Professor [?
Bieberstein, ?] who was a highly respected teacher or professor or something, and his vice-president was a-- was an attorney-- well-respected people.
Without a Judenrat, there would have been complete chaos.
For instance, we had a-- a-- a housing department.
When we moved into the ghetto, there were, I don't know, 30 or 40,000 Jews had to be squeezed in a room for 7,000 people.
Well, somebody had to tell them where they could-- where to go.
It had to be allocated.
There was the-- the work-- labor department, as we could call it.
It was like a city.

Before, the Germans would pick up people from the street to take the labor, and then the Judenrat complained to the gestapo, and we formed the-- the-- the German units had to request people from the-- from the Jewish-- from the ghetto administrator that we need 50 so-and-so, or 20 so-and-so.
And they set up-- the commanders, at least, they knew where the people went and when they came back.
There was the question of food distribution, of administration, and of course of-- of living with the Germans.
And this was a difficult part.
I would have hated to be a Juden-- one of the Juden-- one of the Jewish councilmen.
It was a great responsibility.
Your life was in danger all the time, because the Germans, if you don't do what-- what they want, that's it.
So it-- I think the Jewish Judenrat did as good a job as they could.
It was a no win-- no win situation.
And of course, if you have a hundred rooms to give out and there are 300 people that want a room, the hundred will be happy, but 200 will be unhappy.
And that-- that was the situation.
I was-- I thank God a million times that I was not in-- that I was not in the Judenrat while I worked there in the office and had certain knowledge there.
It-- it-- it was a tough job.
But I think the Jewish-- the Jewish-- the Judenrat people did the best they could, most of them, did the best they could with an impossible-- with an impossible task.
And they did not volunteer to do this.
They were appointed and said, well, you are this, and you go and say no to a German.
There is no such thing.
Well, because they-- they had to negotiate with Germans, sometimes.
The Germans came very often and they came to the Jews when they needed something.
They needed coffee, so you had to get them some coffee and the-- the people in Krakow in the Judenrat, they still had the connections in town to be able to get these things.
And I don't know.
It's-- it's-- it's like-- it's like a city administration in an occupied country.
Let's say you take Paris, when-- when-- when France was occupied by the Germans.
I'm sure the administration was not-- not very well-liked.
Well, my mother worked in a-- that factory-- one of the factories that I think Josef [?
Modridge ?] had.
He was a German-- I think a Viennese German-- who was friendly to us and who-- who supplied us with more food than we could get, and they had hundreds of women working there.
And then we had factories for-- and I don't remember whether it was in ghetto in Plaszow-- made brushes.
[NON-ENGLISH] It was-- you needed-- they made handmade brushes, scrubbing brushes and so on, and the children worked there, you know, the grown up children.
And-- and that was a big thing.
And then we had other workshops, plumbing.
The Jews were very good shoemakers in the ghetto, especially in Krak-- in Plaszow.
They had workshops where-- the Germans liked high boots.
The Jews are excellent makers of these high-- the upper-- upper parts of boots.
And all the SS men, the SS officers all came into Plaszow and got these boots, which the Germans had made for them.
So it was a crazy world, I'm telling you.
It's hard to-- hard to do.
And my father, I don't really remember it.
One time I got him a job in an office in the ghetto, and so did my sister.
And I, frankly, I don't remember really too much unless I'm reading up on it again.
Sure.
What do you remember about the construction of Plaszow, the concentration--
Plaszow?
Well, we knew-- I think it started in January, '43, I think.
We knew it was being built.
It was being built by Jewish labor from the ghetto who had to go out to Plaszow every morning in groups, under German guard [INAUDIBLE].
And they had to build that-- that-- the camp.
The camp was partially built on a Jewish cemetery.
They knocked down the Jewish tombstones-- the tombstones-- and used them for paving roads in the-- in the-- in the camp.
They worked very hard.
There were Jewish architects and Jewish engineers who had to do the designing.
And there was one, Greenberg, who-- who every morning he went out, every night he came back beaten up.
In the morning he had to go out again.
And Goethe was on top of him all the time, and a lot of people got killed during that time.
And my sister had to go and move into the ghetto-- into Plaszow a few weeks before we came out.
Why, I don't remember.
And that's how that [INAUDIBLE].
It was a big area of-- of barracks, about 600 to 800 in each barrack, and the center of the area was what we an Appelplatz, like an assembly square.
And in between were a few latrines-- latrines and-- and-- and a laundry room.
And there was one police building.
We had a police, a Jewish camp police.

And we knew it was coming, and then we had to get-- those in the ghetto had to-- had to get permission again to move out, and-- and who couldn't make it, I don't remember what-- probably didn't get permit-- they were either shot or sent out.
At that time, there was, I think hundreds of children in the ghetto who were-- who were shot, and-- and older people, too.
Well, no furniture or anything like this.
You could take a suitcase or anything like this.
But then, we had no room where to put it, the things, because we had-- the barracks had 600 or 800 people.
No-- no bunks, even.
We had individual platforms-- planks, in three tiers.
And at one point, we had-- I just read this-- we had 28 centimeter of sleeping space per person, which is about-- a little less than 12 inches.
That meant when-- when one turned around, the-- the other one had to turn around.
And it was very bad.
That was really bad until it got organized.

There was a lot of cruelty, a lot of-- a lot of beating and a lot of-- I remember in the-- the first few days while I was not allotted to any particular place, I was hanging around the office, they were-- they would take Jews and hang them on their feet up on the wall, and with their head down, and would beat them up.
Or hang them up-- had their hands with their feet down, and with whips.
And so much shooting.
I remember one of the first incidents, I-- we stand-- stood on the assembly square where we had to go two or three times a day to be counted.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stood, next to me, and [INAUDIBLE], was a fellow by the name of Raab, R-A-A-B, who-- a nice fellow.
He was an office worker, too.
And he stepped out of line, and the sergeant came up and shot him, just-- just for that.
And I remember the name of the sergeant was [?
Stoyevsky. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I passed out at this point, and they took me to the sick-- sick bay, which in itself was dangerous.
And I got through it and it was all right.

And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had to be counted three times-- two or three times a day, where we stood there by barracks and the-- each barrack has a-- had a barrack elder, like one Jew in charge of the barrack who was responsible for-- for the barrack.
Like each work group had a capo, a head man, and he was responsible.

I think [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the ghetto-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] the ghetto was liquidated on March 13th, '43, so that must be the day, or a few days before-- before we got into the-- into the camp.
A hundred people remained to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to clean up.
And on this day, hundreds of children and grown ups stayed in the ghetto, as they were not permitted to go in the work-- walk to the camp-- and shot.
And that's that was March-- around March 13, '43, I think.
In Plaszow?
Well again, I stood around for awhile, and again, I found a job in-- in the office.
It was a type-- they call in German, called it Schreiber.
Maybe you call it a scribe.
Screeb?
A scribe?
What do you think?
Taking correspondence and office-- office jobs.
I usually worked for the Schutzhaftlagerfuhrer.
The-- the ghetto, the camp was-- had different subdivisions, like-- like-- like a [INAUDIBLE].
One was a Schutzhaftlagerfuhrer, actually, [INAUDIBLE] protective custody-- custody camp, but it was not for our protection.
It was the-- the sleeping quarters of the-- of the people.
And another area were-- were the workshops, where the work was, and another area, where the administration buildings were, and the other areas where the Germans lived.
So I was in cha-- I worked with the man, whoever he was at that time, who was in charge.
It was usually a cap-- lieutenant or a first lieutenant who-- who was in charge, and I worked in his office.
Well, you had to be on their [INAUDIBLE].
They-- you could-- you were not allowed to know everything, yet I had to know everything.
That's why I learned to-- to read letters upside down, or-- or read carbon papers where you see-- because if they couldn't find anything, they come to you.
I was slapped a few times.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember one incident where we had a fairly decent man there, his name of Grimm, G-R-I-M-M.
I took the dictation.
I stood in front of his desk, or I sat in front of his desk, and I fell asleep with a pencil in my hand.
Now with another man, that would have been the end, but he-- and I sat at-- we had-- we had a big problem with-- with bugs and fleas, bedbugs and fleas.
When you have hundreds of bedbugs at night, you don't sleep.
And if you're undernourished and you don't have enough food.
And I told him that I couldn't sleep at night, I'm busy with bedbugs.
And he transferred me to another barrack, which was a little better.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I managed to get-- get along with them, maybe because I was good in what I did.
I-- in other words, I was able to write the letters that they wanted, and when they wanted it.
It was not an easy job, because-- it sounds like a cushy job-- gee, you work at an office when it's cold outside-- it wasn't like this, because you were under their eyes all the time.
You had no office hours, no regular hours.
They could come at 1:00 at night and pull you out, and come to the office, and you work all through for two days and nights without coming back to your barrack again if they wanted to do this.
It was not easy at all.
My mother worked in the-- in one of the clothes-- the Plaszow factory.
And she worked harder than all of us because she had to work night shift.
And that was-- she was, by that time, a woman in her 50s, and-- with white hair.
And that was not easy, and a lot of things happened.
And at one time, there was 5,000 women from night shift were transported out to other camps, and somehow, by a miracle, my mother was not taken.
Some other time in transport was, we would hide our parents in our barracks under our little pillows that we had, and we would just hide them.
Or when the-- if people were called by barrack and when we knew they were in barrack number 13, and then we took them into barrack number 8, they'd know she-- she wasn't there.
So once you got-- once you-- they didn't find you for-- for an action, that was all right.
Then you-- then you were back again, and all right.
The question was to survive this one day or this one hour.
She did the same thing, and later on, I-- she had a job in one of the office sections, too.
[INAUDIBLE] with the ghetto [INAUDIBLE].
My father did the same thing.
My father worked outside the camp for awhile.
We had this [GERMAN] commando that that out-- outside groups that got taken out under guard into town every morning, and then they came back every night.
And they had an office that-- that they called it the Confiscated Jewish Property-- artwork, and all these things.

They had an office on this first-- in the-- in the camp where he worked, and then this office went out-- outside the camp in a school, in [NON-ENGLISH] Krakow, the name of the street, where they had an office.
They went out in the morning and they came back at night.
And one incident from this, there was a Professor [?
Dreiblat. ?] He worked in the office with us, and I knew him from the ghetto.
And he-- he worked on this place outside.
And one day, he disappeared.
And that was dangerous.
If somebody disappeared in town from that, it-- it was very dangerous.
And he was originally a mathematics professor.
And where he worked in the office with-- with us, he was always doodling.
He was pulled out by the Resistance-- by the Polish Resistance.
He developed-- he designed bombs, handmade bombs for the underground.
And that was-- that group came back in the-- in the-- in the afternoon from town, and they didn't let them into the camp.
They stood at the wire-- at the barbed wire-- for a few hours.
We knew something was wrong.
And again a miracle happened.
They let everybody in, because other occasions, they-- they shot the whole commando then.
Eh, what food?
[LAUGHS] We got soup, little soup, and sometimes [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we got a little bread, and that was about it.
Yes.
Yes, there was.
The people who work outside the camp, usually, they're-- either for money or through people that they had-- there were connections.
See, the-- the Plaszow was near Krakow, and it had-- there was still a base.
Most of the people in Plaszow were from Krakow, where there was still a connection, a support base outside.
But it was bad.
We had-- we didn't-- when I was liberated, I weighed 85 pounds.
And that was already after-- after six good months in Schindler's camp.
Yes.
Well, there were German doctors and were Jewish doctors.
The German doctors, a lot of people were killed there.
They experimented with them.
No, I don't.
I don't, but I know that people who went into the-- into the sick bay and never came out again.
And of course, there was no medic-- medicine, no medications, and Jewish doctors could do only so much, and--
Yes, because he always came to visit Goeth.
I saw Schindler there.
I didn't know him personally, or I didn't have much contact with him.
I saw Schindler.
At one point, and I'm not sure whether it was with Schindler, my German boss was a Lieutenant John, J-O-H-N, and he said to me he had to go and inspect one of the satellite camps.
And I think it may have been Schindler's camp.
I'm not sure.
I should come with him, so I went out with him.
He took me out in his car, into town, out of the camp, in my uniform.
No, I didn't have a uniform then, it was just-- and at one point-- at one point, he stopped.
He said, 'I have to go to the post office.
I'll be right back.' And he left me alone in the car.
Now, why he did it, I don't know, because I could have gotten out of that car and I could have just walked away.
Of course, it was out of the question for me, because I had my family in the camp.
Whether he wanted to test me, or whether he wanted-- was just stupid, I don't know.
But that was a strange-- a strange experience.
And then we went to one of these camps where the people lived there, where they had satellite camps, and then we went back.
I have-- yeah.
Go ahead.
I think we wore whatever we have left-- had left over from-- from what we brought into the camp.
There were warehouses and warehouses with clothing and shoes of the people who were killed-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] big warehouses.
So there was enough clothes if you wanted it, and-- but we were-- we were cold.
Winter is cold.
And a couple of things, couple of incidences I'd like to-- I'd like to tell you.
My mother, my sister were-- there were women's barracks and separate barracks-- my mother had dentures.
She had no teeth.
She was a white-haired woman in her '50s.
We knew Plaszow was going to be liquidated.
And a few weeks before that, she woke up in the morning and she was on the bottom layer of the thing, and she had kept her teeth under her-- under the pane, and her teeth were gone.
And everything we searched, were not there.
It was probably a rat or a mice that-- that carried them away.
And that was a desperate situation.
And we knew a young dentist.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He-- we told him about it.
He worked as a dentist for the Ukrainian [INAUDIBLE] company which we had.
There were these [?
Volksdeutsche, ?] these ethnic Germans who worked in there.
He worked for them as a dentist.
He smuggled impression matter-- material out of-- out of his office where he worked, and that was dangerous already.
He came into my mother's barrack.
He-- he made an impression.
He smuggled it out with the Ukrainians' work into town to a dental lab, and about a week or 10 days later, he came back with a new set of teeth for my mother.
It was about a week or 10 days before she was sent to Auschwitz.
Now, there was not the slightest chance at all that she could have survived Auschwitz-- not the slightest chance.
A white-haired woman and without teeth?
Forget it.
It's a remarkable thing for me, this.
It shows, first of all, the incredible conditions under which we lived.
You know, things here, you lose your teeth, you go to the dentist.
You know, this was a matter of life and death, not having your teeth.
And that there was man-- one man-- who took a chance.
He risked his own life to help this woman.
He didn't get paid for it.
He didn't ask for anything.
And this, to me, was a very remarkable feel.
And these things did happen.
With Goeth?
Well, I-- I saw Goeth, he would come into the-- our office and-- --to talk about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] talk to one of our officers.
So we saw him.
